task.title: Characterize the bare-vs-suffixed Slack channel-id bug class

events.content: Generalize: events carry the BARE channel id, mounts use the SUFFIXED <id>__<slug> form; the mismatch is a recurring bug class fixed in two layers

raw.question: Is the bare-vs-suffixed channel id a one-off, or a systemic id mismatch?
raw.chosen: Treat it as a systemic class: bridge/dispatch layer (pear#155 + cloud#1995) AND fs/events layer (cloud#2010) each independently needed the suffixed form
alternatives: one-off fix at the preview renderer only
normalize ids at read time inside Pear
raw.reasoning: Slack integration events emit the BARE channel id, while the mount root and every alias subtree use the SUFFIXED <id>__<slug> form. Any consumer that joins event ids to mount paths by literal prefix breaks. This surfaced twice: (a) the 'content unavailable' preview bug + the original thread-routing bug, fixed at the bridge/dispatch layer via pear#155 + cloud#1995; and (b) thread reply events dropped by the daemon's literal-prefix filter, fixed at the fs/events emit layer via cloud#2010. Durable lesson: normalize to the suffixed path at EVERY event emit site; never compare bare vs suffixed by literal prefix.

retrospective.summary: The bare-vs-suffixed channel-id mismatch (events bare, mounts suffixed <id>__<slug>) is a systemic class, not a one-off. Fixed at the bridge layer (pear#155/cloud#1995) and again at the fs/events layer (cloud#2010). Rule: emit the suffixed path at every source; never literal-prefix-compare bare vs suffixed.
retrospective.approach: When the same id-shape mismatch bites a second time, stop point-fixing and characterize the class + enumerate every emit/compare site.
